## Summary

This March 2026 guide from Incremys is the most comprehensive and data-grounded treatment of meta description optimization in the research set. It opens with a 2025 Semrush statistic that anchors the strategic context: "60% of searches end without a click," meaning each SERP snippet has become a micro landing page competing for shrinking click-to-site conversions. The implication is that meta description craft has become more important, not less, as zero-click rates climb.

The guide provides a concrete writing framework for high-performing meta descriptions: a 4-element structure of (1) Promise - what the page helps achieve; (2) Proof - a factual element actually on the page (stat, number, template, date); (3) Benefit - the user outcome; (4) CTA - an action verb if it stays natural. The "Proof" element is particularly important because it prevents Google from rewriting the snippet by demonstrating that the meta accurately reflects page content.

On length, the guide reconciles conflicting advice: aim for 156 characters on desktop (approximately 930 pixels); on mobile, often stay below 130 characters. The practical rule is to front-load key information in the first 8-10 words. A notable benchmark: "an optimised description can increase CTR by +43%" (MyLittleBigWeb 2026) - though the guide appropriately notes this is indicative rather than guaranteed.

Google Search Central documentation (updated 2026-02-09) is cited to clarify that Google uses the provided meta when it "describes the page better than other sections" - so precision and specificity are more valuable than clever copywriting.

## Key data points / quotes

- "60% of searches end without a click" - Semrush 2025.
- Optimized meta description can increase CTR by "+43%" - MyLittleBigWeb 2026 (treat as indicative benchmark).
- 4-element framework: Promise + Proof + Benefit + CTA.
- Length: ~156 characters desktop (~930 pixels); ≤130 characters mobile.
- Front-load test: "If you only read the first 8-10 words, do you understand the page's value?"
- Google Search Central (updated 2026-02-09): Google uses provided meta when it "describes the page better than other sections."
- "Avoid empty phrases like 'Welcome to...' which do not help users decide."
- Add concrete differentiator: update year, scope ("for B2B SaaS"), deliverables ("includes templates, checklist"), constraints ("no-code").

## Implications for stinger-forge

- The 4-element Promise + Proof + Benefit + CTA framework is the canonical writing formula for `guides/02-title-h1-meta.md` - it's more actionable than generic "be compelling" advice.
- The "60% zero-click" stat reframes meta description optimization as defensive (protecting remaining click share) rather than just offensive (increasing CTR).
- The front-load-first-8-10-words rule is a testable, practical guideline for the meta description section.
- The specificity instruction (year, scope, deliverables) should become a checklist item in the meta description section.
- Length ranges (156 desktop / ≤130 mobile) should be presented as ranges with the mobile-first caveat, not as a single number.
